subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 5 periods = 200 minutes
Total: 5 periods · 3h 20min

periods.activity_title: Unlikely Friends: Pre-Reading and Story Entry

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pre-reading: complete friendship sentences in shapes
Discuss meaning of 'unlikely' and unlikely friends

phases.description: Open the chapter by asking students what makes a friendship surprising or unexpected. Students share brief personal experiences before completing 'With my friend I…' and 'With my friend I feel…' sentences in the shapes on page 39, then share with classmates.

Read the title 'The Unlikely Best Friends' aloud together. In small groups students discuss the meaning of 'unlikely' (page 39) and then name some unlikely friends from stories or real life, reporting back to the class.
Teacher reads the opening section aloud, pausing to question why Gajaraj feels lonely despite his royal comforts, helping students anticipate the dog's arrival.
Quick whole-class share-out: one sentence each on what makes Gajaraj's loneliness believable. Teacher previews the friendship activities coming in the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: This is the opening period of the chapter; no prior session to transition from. The prose_summary notes that Gajaraj's loneliness despite royal comforts is the emotional hook — use it to anchor the pre-reading discussion. The remaining RFC tasks (task_index 2 onwards) will be addressed in Period 2.

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write activities Gajaraj and Buntee did together
Mid-story discuss: why farmer hugged Buntee
End-story discuss: why Gajaraj refused food

phases.description: Brief recap: teacher asks one student to retell how Buntee arrived at the stable, connecting to Period 1's pre-reading activation.

Students talk about the things Gajaraj and Buntee did together — bathing at the pond, the trunk spray, riding on Gajaraj's back — and write activities in the shapes on page 42. Pairs share their filled shapes aloud.

Teacher reads Part I close and poses the mid-story discussion questions on page 42: why the farmer hugged Buntee, and whether Buntee will leave Gajaraj. Students discuss in groups of three before a whole-class debrief.

Teacher reads the separation episode and asks the end-of-story questions on page 45: why Gajaraj refused food, and who were friends at the end of the story. Students give oral responses; teacher notes key points on the board.

periods.teacher_notes: Continuing from Period 1's story entry; today covers the emotional core of the narrative — separation and grief. The prose_summary highlights that both animals refuse food after separation, which is the most discussion-rich moment. Task_index 5 onwards (extract-based passage questions) are left as self-study; direct students to pages 45–46 for independent exploration.

periods.activity_title: Unlikely Friends: Listening Story and Friend Profile

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Fill missing details in cow-leopard paragraph from listening
Number sentences in correct order after second listening
Find common likes and dislikes; describe friend to class

phases.description: Teacher sets context: the listening passage tells a real story of an unlikely friendship between a cow and a leopard from Vadodara. Students listen for the first time and fill in the missing details in the paragraph on page 49.
Teacher reads the passage again; students number the sentences in correct order on page 50, comparing with a partner before a whole-class check.
Transition to speaking: students interview a partner to find common likes and dislikes, completing the 'I am / I like / My friend is / My friend likes / Both of us like' frame.

Students stand and describe their friend to the class using the cue sentences provided: 'My friend and I like to play…', 'I like to ___ but my friend likes to ___', 'We are different as…'. Teacher circulates and prompts quieter students.

Class debrief: what made the cow-leopard friendship as 'unlikely' as Gajaraj and Buntee's? Students offer one sentence each linking the listening passage to the narrative. Teacher previews the writing period.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition from RFC periods; listening and speaking are paired today per NCF guidance that these skills should occur together. The transcript (page 71) must be read aloud clearly twice; the leopard-cow story from Vadodara parallels the Jataka tale's cross-species friendship theme closely.

periods.activity_title: Writing About a Friend: Similarities and Differences

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write six sentences about friend showing similarities and differences

phases.description: Quick recall of the speaking activity from Period 3: teacher asks two volunteers to share what they found similar and different about their friend. These ideas feed directly into the writing task.
Teacher models writing two sample sentences on the board using the connector words given (same, different, but, both, neither, nor), showing how to combine similarity and difference in one sentence.

Students independently write six sentences about their friend, using the facts they gathered in the speaking task and the connector words as scaffolds. Teacher circulates and provides individual feedback.
Two or three students read their sentences aloud. Class responds with a 'thumbs up' if the sentences capture both similarity and difference. Teacher previews the vocabulary period.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition from the speaking task in Period 3 — the friend-profile data students collected there is the direct input for today's writing. Beyond-text tasks for Section A are left as self-study; point students to page 51 for the Tarra and Bella news report and the Jataka exploration on their own time.

periods.activity_title: Opposites, Crossword, and Three Forms of Past Tense

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Complete crossword with opposites from the story
Sort story verbs into simple past, past progressive, past perfect

homework.task_brief: Let us learn (p.49): Select correct past-tense form to complete passage on farmer and Buntee

phases.description: Teacher explains how opposites can be formed by adding prefixes (un-, in-, dis-, mis-) using 'unhappy' and 'incorrect' as anchors, then invites students to brainstorm story words. Students complete the crossword puzzle on page 47 independently.

Pairs check crossword answers. Teacher introduces the three forms of past tense (Simple Past, Past Progressive, Past Perfect) with brief examples from the story text itself, writing the column headers on the board.
Students individually sort the given story verbs (was looking, looked, gave, had ordered, etc.) into the three-column table on page 48. Pairs then compare and justify their choices to each other.

Teacher leads whole-class confirmation of the sorted verbs and clarifies common confusions between Simple Past and Past Progressive. Homework task on page 49 (selecting correct tense for the farmer-Buntee passage) is set and explained.

periods.teacher_notes: Vocabulary_grammar is isolated in its own period per Rule 4. The crossword task is grounded in story vocabulary, making it a seamless extension of the narrative study from Periods 1–2. Task_index 1 (paragraph fill using crossword words) and task_index 4 (neither/nor practice) are left as self-study; alert students to page 47 and page 49 respectively.

items.source_lo: Student interprets events and emotional relationships in a narrative text.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Unlikely Best Friends', both Gajaraj and Buntee refuse all food after they are separated. Read the sentences below and mark each one TRUE or FALSE.
(1) Gajaraj stopped eating because the mahout forgot to bring his food.
(2) Buntee refused food because he missed Gajaraj after being taken away by the farmer.
(3) The farmer realised something was wrong with Buntee by observing that Buntee did not eat.
(4) The mahout suspected that Gajaraj was unhappy because of the absent dog.

options.text: Gajaraj stopped eating because the mahout forgot to bring his food.
Buntee refused food because he missed Gajaraj after being taken away by the farmer.
The farmer realised something was wrong with Buntee by observing that Buntee did not eat.
The mahout suspected that Gajaraj was unhappy because of the absent dog.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. False — Gajaraj refused food out of grief for Buntee, not because the mahout neglected him. 2. True — Buntee refused food because he missed Gajaraj after being taken away by the farmer. 3. True — The farmer realised something was wrong when he saw that Buntee would not eat. 4. True — The mahout suspected Gajaraj was unhappy because of the absent dog.

items.question_text:
In 'The Unlikely Best Friends', both Gajaraj and Buntee refuse all food after they are separated. Read the sentences below and mark each one TRUE or FALSE.
(1) Gajaraj stopped eating because the mahout forgot to bring his food.
(2) Buntee refused food because he missed Gajaraj after being taken away by the farmer.
(3) The farmer realised something was wrong with Buntee by observing that Buntee did not eat.
(4) The mahout suspected that Gajaraj was unhappy because of the absent dog.

items.source_lo: Student recalls sequence and specific details from a spoken narrative.
items.item_stem:
You will listen to a story about a cow and a leopard from Vadodara who became unlikely friends. After listening, number the following events (1–4) in the order they happened in the story.
(a) The villagers were surprised the leopard never attacked the cow.
(b) The leopard grew older and left the village.
(c) The cow took care of the leopard cub like a mother.
(d) The leopard began sitting quietly beside the cow at night.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: The correct sequence is: (c) 1 — the cow cared for the cub first; (b) 2 — the leopard grew up and left; (d) 3 — it returned to sit beside the cow at night; (a) 4 — the villagers were ultimately surprised by their friendship.

items.question_text:
You will listen to a story about a cow and a leopard from Vadodara who became unlikely friends. After listening, number the following events (1–4) in the order they happened in the story.
(a) The villagers were surprised the leopard never attacked the cow.
(b) The leopard grew older and left the village.
(c) The cow took care of the leopard cub like a mother.
(d) The leopard began sitting quietly beside the cow at night.

items.source_lo: Student describes similarities and differences between self and a friend orally.
items.item_stem: Gajaraj and Buntee were very different animals — one was a royal elephant and the other a stray dog — yet they found joy in being together. Think about yourself and a close friend. In 2–3 minutes, speak to your partner about: (a) one activity you both enjoy, (b) one way you are different from each other, and (c) why the difference does not affect your friendship. Use connecting words such as 'both', 'but', 'while', and 'still' in your response.

expected_elements: Names at least one shared activity clearly.
States one genuine difference using a contrast word (but/while/still).
Explains why the difference does not weaken the friendship.
Speaks in complete sentences with acceptable fluency.

items.question_text: Gajaraj and Buntee were very different animals — one was a royal elephant and the other a stray dog — yet they found joy in being together. Think about yourself and a close friend. In 2–3 minutes, speak to your partner about: (a) one activity you both enjoy, (b) one way you are different from each other, and (c) why the difference does not affect your friendship. Use connecting words such as 'both', 'but', 'while', and 'still' in your response.

items.source_lo: Student writes sentences comparing a friend using contrast connectors.
items.item_stem: Gajaraj and Buntee were alike in some ways — both loved being together and both felt deep sadness when separated — yet they were very different in size, species, and background. Think of a friend of yours. Write 5–6 sentences describing how you and your friend are both similar and different. Use at least THREE of the following connecting words in your sentences: both, but, unlike, still, neither, while.

expected_elements: Mentions at least one genuine similarity between writer and friend.
States at least one clear difference, not just a physical detail.
Uses three or more contrast/comparison connectors correctly.
Writes in complete, grammatically complete sentences.

items.question_text: Gajaraj and Buntee were alike in some ways — both loved being together and both felt deep sadness when separated — yet they were very different in size, species, and background. Think of a friend of yours. Write 5–6 sentences describing how you and your friend are both similar and different. Use at least THREE of the following connecting words in your sentences: both, but, unlike, still, neither, while.

items.source_lo: Student uses three forms of past tense correctly in story context.
items.item_stem:
The passage below describes events from 'The Unlikely Best Friends'. Choose the correct form of the verb from the brackets to fill each blank. Each blank tests one of the three past tense forms: Simple Past, Past Progressive, or Past Perfect.

The mahout (1) _____________ (noticed / was noticing / had noticed) that Gajaraj's mood changed the moment a stray dog entered the stable. By the time the farmer arrived, Gajaraj (2) _____________ (shared / was sharing / had shared) his food with the dog for several days. When the farmer led Buntee away, Gajaraj (3) _____________ (stood / was standing / had stood) motionless at the stable door. Neither the mahout nor the farmer (4) _____________ (realised / was realising / had realised) at first that both animals (5) _____________ (refused / were refusing / had refused) their food at exactly the same time.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. noticed (Simple Past — single completed observation); 2. had shared (Past Perfect — action completed before farmer's arrival); 3. stood (Simple Past — single moment action); 4. realised (Simple Past — completed mental action); 5. were refusing (Past Progressive — ongoing action at that point in time).

items.question_text:
The passage below describes events from 'The Unlikely Best Friends'. Choose the correct form of the verb from the brackets to fill each blank. Each blank tests one of the three past tense forms: Simple Past, Past Progressive, or Past Perfect.

The mahout (1) _____________ (noticed / was noticing / had noticed) that Gajaraj's mood changed the moment a stray dog entered the stable. By the time the farmer arrived, Gajaraj (2) _____________ (shared / was sharing / had shared) his food with the dog for several days. When the farmer led Buntee away, Gajaraj (3) _____________ (stood / was standing / had stood) motionless at the stable door. Neither the mahout nor the farmer (4) _____________ (realised / was realising / had realised) at first that both animals (5) _____________ (refused / were refusing / had refused) their food at exactly the same time.
